title: The Indispensable Power That Can't Afford Its Own Leverage
subhead: Turkey sits at the structural centre of every major crisis axis — Iran war mediation, NATO's European future, Gulf security architecture, Eastern Mediterranean energy — yet its economic base is being hollowed out by the very conflicts that make it indispensable. The window to convert temporary indispensability into permanent institutional power is closing faster than Ankara is moving.

sections.heading: Iran War: The Mediator Who Lost the Room to Pakistan
sections.body:
Turkey's strategic position on the Iran war is defined by a paradox: it gained more structural leverage from the conflict than any other regional power, yet it has been outmanoeuvred in the mediation race by Pakistan — a state with a fraction of Turkey's economic weight but superior positioning for the specific mechanics of shuttle diplomacy between Washington and Tehran.

Ankara closed its airspace to US forces operating against Iran, publicly condemned the US-Israel campaign, and positioned itself as the 'adult in the room' — Foreign Minister Fidan's own formulation at Oxford (Middle East Eye). Turkey's airspace closure was not symbolic; it forced operational rerouting that added hours to US sorties and constrained the tempo of strikes during the initial phase of Operation Epic Fury. This was real leverage, extracted at real cost to the bilateral relationship with Washington.

But Pakistan seized the mediation mantle. The 'Islamic Arab Quartet' — Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Egypt — convened in Riyadh on 19 March and Islamabad on 29 March, followed by a meeting on the margins of the Antalya Diplomacy Forum in April (Arab Progress Center). Pakistan brokered the 8 April truce and hosted the 11-12 April talks between US VP JD Vance and Iranian parliamentary speaker Qalibaf. Pakistan's advantages were structural: a direct land border with Iran, no US bases on its territory, a Shia population of 40 million providing credibility with Tehran, and a rising relationship with the Trump administration built through direct engagement between Army Chief Asim Munir and Washington (Arab Progress Center). Qatar and Oman, the traditional mediators, were neutralised — Qatar by its vulnerability to Iranian retaliation given US bases on its soil, Oman by its refusal to engage in open military-conflict mediation.

Turkey remains part of the Quartet framework and Fidan continues to push the 'regional ownership' thesis — 'either we come together and solve our own problems, or an external hegemon will impose solutions that serve its interests' (Middle East Eye). But Pakistan is the channel both Washington and Tehran trust for back-channel communication. Turkey's leverage lies in its NATO membership and its ability to constrain Western military operations; Pakistan's lies in its border geography and its non-aligned credibility. These are complementary, not competitive — but only one state is hosting the actual negotiations.

The MENAFN/Asia Times assessment that 'Turkey is the Iran war's biggest winner without firing a shot' captures the structural gain but misses the urgency: wartime indispensability expires when the shooting stops. Every day that Turkey fails to institutionalise its role — in post-conflict security arrangements, Hormuz freedom-of-navigation frameworks, or a regional security architecture — is a day Pakistan's diplomatic capital compounds while Turkey's depreciates.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey extracted maximum leverage from its geography and alliance position during the active combat phase, but mediation is the currency that buys post-war structural power. Pakistan is converting wartime indispensability into a permanent role as the interlocutor between Washington and Tehran. Turkey's failure to secure a comparable institutional position before the truce hardens into a settlement means it will have borne the economic costs of the war — energy inflation, trade disruption, currency pressure — without capturing the diplomatic capital that converts those costs into lasting strategic position.

sections.heading: Energy and Economy: The Structural Vulnerability No Neutrality Can Fix
sections.body:
Turkey's energy vulnerability is not a temporary shock — it is a permanent structural deficit. A country that imports roughly 90% of its energy, with 15% of its natural gas flowing through a pipeline from a warzone and another significant share transiting a strait controlled by the IRGC Navy, has a security deficit that no diplomatic positioning can offset. The decisive move is not 'weather the shock' — it is to convert crisis dependency into strategic leverage through infrastructure control and supply diversification that locks in alternative routes before the war ends and the political window closes.

The data is unambiguous. Turkey's annual energy import bill runs approximately $65 billion; every $10/barrel increase in oil adds $4.5-5 billion to the current-account deficit (TEPAV). Household electricity and gas prices rose 25% in April (EPDK/BOTAŞ via Bianet). Diesel in Istanbul rose from 60 liras/litre on 2 March to nearly 80 liras by end of April. Annual inflation stood at 30.87% in March — the lowest in years in headline terms — but transportation costs rose over 4.5% in a single month, and food inflation remained above the general rate (Bianet). The CBRT's April PPK summary flagged rising inflation uncertainty driven by energy and food prices.

The Iran-Turkey gas pipeline contract — supplying 9.6 bcm/year from the South Pars field — expires in July, with no negotiations underway for extension (LinkedIn/PGJ Online). Energy Minister Bayraktar denied reports that Iranian gas flows had been cut after US strikes on the Pars field, claiming flows continue and storage is 71% full (Bianet). But a contract expiring in the middle of a war with no replacement negotiation is not a supply question — it is a structural signal that the Iran supply channel is being treated as sunset, not cornerstone.

BOTAŞ is conducting engineering studies for a gas pipeline to northern Cyprus (SigmaLive). TotalEnergies signed a preliminary agreement with TPAO for Black Sea exploration on 13 April (Reuters/TotalEnergies). The earlier TPAO-ExxonMoU covering Black Sea and Mediterranean exploration (Forbes, January 2026) extends the pattern. These are real diversification moves — but Black Sea gas is years from production, and a Cyprus pipeline is a geopolitical statement more than a supply solution.

TEPAV's analysis identified the cascading vulnerabilities: Gulf states supply up to 25% of Turkey's 6 million tonnes/year of chemical fertiliser; aluminium imports from Bahrain, Qatar and UAE reach $1 billion annually; monoethylene glycol feedstock from Gulf plants covers 40% of Turkish market. Turkish exports to the Gulf fell roughly 40% since the war's outbreak, according to TİM president Gültepe (AGBI). Factories in petrochemicals and packaging have halted production and sent staff on holidays (Phoenix Consultancy via AGBI).

CBRT sold gold at the war's outbreak to provide liquidity to defend the lira (Middlefield, April 2026). The lira stands at 44.50 to the dollar (Bianet). These are not cyclical pressures — they are the costs of structural dependency on supply chains that run through a warzone Turkey cannot control.
sections.whyItMatters: The energy shock is not the crisis — it is the revelation of a permanent condition. Turkey's 15% gas dependency on Iran and its reliance on Gulf petrochemical supply chains create a structural vulnerability that neutrality cannot mitigate. Every alternative supply route — Black Sea gas, Cyprus interconnection, Turkmen pipeline — requires years to build and political capital to lock in. The decisive move is to use the wartime urgency as political cover for infrastructure investments that would be politically impossible in peacetime: accelerating nuclear at Akkuyu (where financing for reactors 1-2 is secured, with Russian cooperation per Bayraktar), fast-tracking Black Sea development with TotalEnergies and Exxon, and converting the Cyprus pipeline from an engineering study into a geopolitical fait accompli. The trap is treating this as a price shock to be managed rather than a structural deficit to be permanently reduced.

sections.heading: NATO and European Defence: The Alliance Anchor That PESCO Cuts
sections.body:
Turkey's exclusion from EU defence frameworks is not a diplomatic inconvenience — it is a structural amputation. Every defence architecture that consolidates without Turkey makes decisions about the Eastern Mediterranean, the Black Sea, and the Middle Eastern threat environment without Turkish input, while expecting Turkish forces and geography to underwrite the outcomes. The Ankara NATO Summit in July is the window to force the issue into institutional form.

Defense Minister Güler delivered Ankara's clearest warning yet on 9 April: 'Turkey is no longer a flank country on NATO's southeastern periphery — it is a central ally capable of generating security across the entire European theater' (Defense News). He announced Turkey will assume command of NATO's Allied Reaction Force from 2028 to 2030. The structural argument is unanswerable: Turkey's Kürecik radar provides ballistic missile early warning coverage 'substantially deeper than Romania-based alternatives' — without it, European BMD against Iranian missiles starts from Romania, losing critical intercept opportunity (Brig. Gen. Fazla, STRASAM, via Defense News). Approximately 50 American nuclear weapons at Incirlik function as a concrete security guarantee that no European state can substitute.

But the PESCO wall remains. Turkey's application to join the Military Mobility project stalled due to Greek and Cypriot objections. The European Defence Fund operates on unanimity — giving Athens and Nicosia a structural veto over Turkish participation. As Fazla put it: 'Greece and Cyprus view Turkey's integration into EU defence architecture not as a contribution to their own security, but as the collapse of their strategy for balancing Turkey within the EU' (Defense News). The Atlantic Council's NATO spending tracker shows European allies outpacing expectations ahead of the Ankara summit, but spending without Turkish integration means more capabilities in architectures that exclude Turkey.

Von der Leyen stated on 20 April that 'the European continent must be completed to exclude the influence of Russia, China, and Turkey' (Anadolu Agency via SpecialEurasia). Lumping Turkey with Russia and China as entities to be excluded from Europe is a declaration — not that Turkey is outside Europe, but that parts of Europe prefer the cost of structural exclusion over the price of inclusion. Former Israeli PM Bennett's formulation of Turkey as 'the new Iran' (17 February) and the Netanyahu 'Hexagon Alliance' (Israel-India-Greece-Cypress-'moderate' Arab states) represent an active effort to diplomatically isolate Turkey as a systemic threat (SpecialEurasia).

Trump's posture — questioning NATO, weighing troop reductions in Europe, describing the alliance as a 'paper tiger' — cuts both ways for Ankara. It increases Turkey's value to eastern flank states that need an alternative security provider, but it also weakens the Article 5 umbrella that is Turkey's primary deterrent guarantee. As Kadir Has University's Güvenç noted, 'Turkey understands that as a member of NATO with an Article 5 commitment, its deterrence is far stronger than anything Turkey could achieve alone' (Defense News).
sections.whyItMatters: PESCO exclusion means that as European defence spending surges — driven by the Trump shock and the Iran war — the capabilities being built will operate in frameworks where Greece and Cyprus can veto Turkish participation while simultaneously using those frameworks to balance against Turkey in the Eastern Mediterranean. The Ankara NATO Summit in July is the only venue where Turkey can demand a formal mechanism — a NATO-level framework that bypasses the EU unanimity trap — to integrate its defence industrial capacity and geographic assets into European security planning. The trap is accepting symbolic recognition (commanding the Allied Reaction Force in 2028-30) without structural access to the spending and planning architectures that will determine force composition and deployment for the next decade.

sections.heading: Defence Industry: The Export Machine That Buys Influence But Not Architecture
sections.body:
Turkey's defence exports are growing at 12% year-on-year — $1.9 billion in Q1 2026, with $8.5 billion in 2025 as the all-time high — but export revenue without institutional security architecture is influence without insurance. Turkey can sell Bayraktar Akinci to Saudi Arabia and ULAQ drone boats to SAMI, but it cannot sell itself into a defence pact that guarantees its own security.

The numbers tell one story: 3,500 companies, 100,000 employees, $30.7 billion in cumulative exports 2020-2025 (AGBI/Daily Sabah). In January, STM explored joint UAV production with Qatar's Barzan Holdings. In February, Saudi SAMI signed with Turkey's ULAQ for joint unmanned surface vehicle development. Baykar's Akinci export deal with Saudi Arabia's Ministry of Defense followed the 2023 Baykar-SAMI localisation agreement. Turkey-Egypt signed a $350 million MKE ammunition and production line deal during Erdogan's February Cairo visit (Middle East Eye). Baykar's TB2 successfully test-flew with its indigenous TM100 engine in April (DefenceWeb). These are real industrial achievements with real strategic effects — defence exports create dependency relationships that translate into political access.

But the 'Islamic Arab Quartet' security pact that Fidan is pushing — Turkey, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Pakistan — remains a 'platform' for cooperation, not a mutual defence commitment (Middle East Eye). Fidan's language is explicit: 'We are exploring how, as countries with a certain degree of influence in the region, we can combine our strengths to solve problems.' This is institutional language for an arrangement that does not yet exist in institutional form. The Saudi-Pakistan mutual defence pact, signed in late 2025, is a bilateral agreement; Turkey was reported to be in advanced talks to join (Reuters, January 2026), but as of April, there is no signature.

The structural problem: defence exports create client relationships; mutual defence pacts create alliance relationships. Client relationships are transactional — they last as long as the technology edge and the price are competitive. Alliance relationships create mutual obligations that persist through political shocks. Turkey has spent a decade building the first and is failing to convert it into the second.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defence industry is its single most effective tool for converting industrial capacity into strategic weight. Every Akinci sold to Riyadh, every TB2 production line in Saudi Arabia, every MKE factory in Cairo creates a dependency relationship that gives Ankara political leverage. But the Iran war has demonstrated that influence without architecture is fragile — Turkey's Gulf clients can buy Turkish drones while simultaneously hosting the US bases from which strikes against Iran are launched. The decisive move is to lock defence-industrial dependency into the formal security pact framework before the war ends and the urgency that drives Gulf security diversification dissipates. The trap is confusing export orders with alliance depth.

sections.heading: Eastern Mediterranean: Pipeline to Northern Cyprus as Geopolitical Fait Accompli
sections.body:
BOTAŞ's engineering study for a gas pipeline to northern Cyprus is not an energy project — it is a territorial claim rendered in steel. In a region where every seabed pipeline is a jurisdictional assertion, connecting mainland Turkey to northern Cyprus via gas infrastructure creates facts on the ground that no diplomatic process can easily reverse.

Energy Minister Bayraktar confirmed the BOTAŞ engineering study and stated the objective of channelling natural gas toward Turkey through a 'unified flow system' (SigmaLive, 30 April). This is the latest move in a long game: Turkey's position in the Eastern Mediterranean has always been that it will not accept an energy architecture in which Greek Cyprus and its partners — Chevron, Eni, TotalEnergies — develop offshore gas while Turkey and the Turkish Cypriots are excluded. President Christodoulides responded by citing UNCLOS and the 1982 Law of the Sea convention, emphasising strong interest from companies in new exploration blocks within Cyprus' EEZ (SigmaLive).

The geopolitical context amplifies the stakes. The Iran war and the Hormuz closure have reinforced the Eastern Mediterranean's role as an alternative energy corridor for Europe — Christodoulides explicitly linked the Ukraine war and regional instability to the Eastern Mediterranean's strategic importance (SigmaLive). TotalEnergies' 13 April MoU with TPAO for Black Sea exploration positions the French major as a partner on both sides of the Turkey-Cyprus divide — a potential bridge or a potential point of leverage, depending on how Ankara plays it.

Israel's 'Hexagon Alliance' — which includes Greece and Cyprus as members — is an explicit effort to construct a regional architecture that bypasses Turkey (SpecialEurasia). IMEC, the India-Middle East-Europe corridor, is the economic infrastructure of that architecture. A Turkish pipeline to northern Cyprus does not merely supply gas — it asserts that any Eastern Mediterranean energy architecture that excludes Turkish Cypriots will face a competing physical infrastructure that makes exclusion practically impossible.
sections.whyItMatters: The Eastern Mediterranean is where Turkey's exclusion from European defence frameworks and its rivalry with Israel converge in physical form. If Greece, Cyprus, and Israel build an energy corridor to Europe that routes around Turkey — as IMEC and the Hexagon Alliance envision — Turkey becomes a bypassed power in its own maritime neighbourhood. The pipeline to northern Cyprus is the countermovement: it forces any future energy settlement to account for Turkish infrastructure and Turkish Cypriot interests as physical facts, not diplomatic claims. The trap is treating the Cyprus pipeline as an energy solution rather than a territorial assertion — if it is built without accompanying diplomatic architecture that translates the physical fact into legal recognition, it remains a contested asset vulnerable to legal challenge and sanction.

sections.heading: Kurdish Peace Process: The Internal Front That Determines External Leverage
sections.body:
The Kurdish peace process is not a domestic reform agenda — it is the prerequisite for Turkey's ability to project power externally without the constant drag of a 40,000-death internal conflict consuming military capacity, political bandwidth, and international credibility. A Turkey that resolves the Kurdish question frees 30-40% of its military from counter-insurgency deployment and removes the single biggest constraint on its European integration.

Erdogan told parliament on 29 April that the process is progressing in a 'positive atmosphere' and that those drawing pessimistic conclusions are 'acting on illusions, not facts' (Reuters). The immediate trigger was the DEM Party's unusually sharp criticism accusing the government of acting in a 'hesitant, timid and stalling manner' despite a 'historic opportunity for peace.' Erdogan's response — 'there is a positive atmosphere, what needs to be done is clear' — suggests the standoff is over sequencing: Ankara insists disarmament must be verified before further legal or political measures; Kurdish political actors demand faster reforms.

The structural context is decisive. The PKK halted attacks last year and announced its decision to disband in May 2025, following Ocalan's February 2025 call. A parliamentary commission has approved a report outlining a roadmap for legal reforms alongside PKK disbandment. This is the furthest the process has ever advanced. But the same political system that produced Erdogan's response also contains the AKP-MHP alliance, where the MHP's nationalist base treats concessions to Kurdish demands as existential. The 'hesitant and stalling' criticism from DEM is a political signal that the Kurdish side sees a closing window — not because the process is failing, but because the government's coalition constraints limit how fast it can move.

The external dimension is critical. Turkey's Kurdish policy is the single largest obstacle to PESCO integration — Greece and Cyprus cite Turkish military operations against Kurdish groups as evidence that Turkey is a threat, not a partner. The EU's refusal to open defence frameworks to Turkey rests partly on the Kurdish question. Resolving it removes the primary political ammunition from the PESCO veto coalition.
sections.whyItMatters: A Turkey still fighting a Kurdish insurgency cannot fully integrate into European defence, cannot credibly position itself as a regional security provider, and cannot deploy its military capacity externally without the constant risk of a two-front scenario. The peace process is not a humanitarian project — it is the single largest structural unlock in Turkish grand strategy. The trap is the sequencing standoff: Ankara's insistence on verified disarmament before reforms mirrors every post-conflict trap where the party with superior military position demands security guarantees before political concessions, only to find that without political concessions, the military position erodes. The decisive move is to break the sequencing deadlock by linking discrete, reversible reform steps to verified disarmament milestones — creating a reciprocal process rather than a sequential one.

sections.heading: Israel Rivalry: The Threat Construction That Becomes Self-Fulfilling
sections.body:
Israel is actively constructing Turkey as its next existential adversary after Iran and Hezbollah — and the structural logic of threat construction in the Middle East is that declared threats tend to become real ones through the arms races, alliance formations, and escalatory rhetoric they trigger. Turkey's NATO membership is the single factor that prevents this from becoming a military confrontation; removing it — through a Trump withdrawal or Article 5 erosion — would remove the last structural barrier to direct friction.

The evidence is mounting. Former PM Bennett defined Turkey as 'the new Iran' on 17 February (SpecialEurasia). Netanyahu's 'Hexagon Alliance' explicitly positions Israel, India, Greece, and Cyprus as a counterweight to Turkish influence. Von der Leyen's 20 April statement grouping Turkey with Russia and China as influences to be excluded from Europe reinforces the diplomatic isolation framework. Turkey suspended all trade with Israel in May 2024 and tightened restrictions in February 2026 by removing customs exemptions for Turkish goods routed through Europe (SpecialEurasia).

The escalatory risk is asymmetric. Israel has nuclear ambiguity and a $25+ billion annual US military assistance pipeline. Turkey has NATO Article 5 and approximately 50 US nuclear weapons at Incirlik — but Trump's stated desire to pull out of NATO, described by former NCTC director Joe Kent as driven partly by a strategic intent to 'dissolve the constraints of Article 5, thereby enabling the United States to intervene in support of Israel in the event of a military confrontation with Turkey' (SpecialEurasia), directly threatens the structural barrier that keeps the rivalry in the diplomatic and economic domains.

Turkey's Pakistan alignment compounds the escalatory dynamic. The Pakistan-Turkey military-strategic relationship — including the December 2025 'Eastern Mediterranean-2025' joint naval drills, mutual support on Kashmir/Cyprus/Nagorno-Karabakh — introduces nuclear-armed Pakistan into the Eastern Mediterranean equation in a way that Israel's previous regional adversaries (Iran, Hezbollah, Hamas) never did (SpecialEurasia).
sections.whyItMatters: The Israel-Turkey rivalry is the most dangerous structural fault line in the Eastern Mediterranean because it pits two US-aligned military powers against each other in a region where the US is the security guarantor for both. The current equilibrium holds because NATO Article 5 makes a direct Turkish-Israeli confrontation existentially irrational for both sides. But the equilibrium is conditional on NATO's credibility — and Trump's threats to withdraw, combined with the 'Hexagon Alliance' architecture that builds alternative security structures excluding Turkey, are actively degrading that credibility. The trap is treating the Israel-Turkey rivalry as a rhetorical phenomenon that can be managed through diplomatic channels. Threat construction is not rhetoric — it is the preparatory phase for conflict. The decisive move is to make NATO Article 5 credibility the explicit centrepiece of the Ankara Summit in July, forcing Washington to publicly commit to the mutual defence guarantee that keeps the rivalry in the non-kinetic domain.

bottomLine: Turkey occupies the structural centre of every major crisis axis in the emerging order — Iran war mediation, European defence integration, Gulf security architecture, Eastern Mediterranean energy, and the Israel rivalry — yet its position in each domain is defined by a gap between temporary indispensability and permanent institutional power. Pakistan is converting wartime mediation into lasting diplomatic capital while Turkey watches from the Quartet. The PESCO wall means European defence spending surges in frameworks that exclude Turkey while relying on Turkish geography and assets. Defence exports buy client relationships but not the mutual defence pact that converts industrial influence into alliance depth. The Kurdish peace process — the single structural unlock for Turkey's external projection — is stalled in a sequencing deadlock that mirrors every post-conflict trap in the literature. The Israel rivalry is entering threat-construction phase at exactly the moment when the NATO guarantee that keeps it non-kinetic is being questioned by the US president. Every one of these domains has a window — and every window is closing. Turkey's strategic problem is not that it lacks leverage; it is that leverage depreciates faster than Ankara is institutionalising it. The state that emerges from the Iran war with the most permanent structural position will be the one that converts temporary indispensability into treaty obligations, infrastructure facts, and alliance frameworks before the shooting stops and the political cover for transformational moves disappears.
sources: Reuters (Erdogan Kurdish peace, 29 Apr 2026), Middle East Eye (Fidan Quartet/security pact, 21 Mar 2026), Defense News (Güler NATO/European defence, 13 Apr 2026), AGBI (defence exports Q1 2026, 3 Apr 2026; TEPAV economic vulnerability, 20 Mar 2026), Bianet (electricity/gas price hikes 25%, Apr 2026; inflation/diesel data), Arab Progress Center/Kawas (Pakistan mediation analysis, 21 Apr 2026), SpecialEurasia (Turkey-Israel rivalry, 25 Apr 2026), SigmaLive (Cyprus pipeline BOTAŞ, 30 Apr 2026), TotalEnergies/TPAO MoU (13 Apr 2026), Forbes (Exxon-TPAO Black Sea, Jan 2026), MENAFN/Asia Times (Turkey Iran war winner), Wikipedia (2026 Ankara NATO Summit, 7-8 Jul; Strait of Hormuz crisis), Atlantic Council (NATO spending tracker), Middlefield (CBRT gold sales), LinkedIn/PGJ Online (Iran-Turkey gas contract expiry July), Al Jazeera (Pakistan truce mediation, 8 Apr 2026), Anadolu Agency (von der Leyen Turkey-Russia-China grouping, 20 Apr 2026), DefenceWeb (Baykar TM100 engine test)
